\bottomrule

\caption{Coverage design for the 1,000 controlled natural-language ordering requests. The dimensions are used to reduce template bias and cover realistic menu-ordering situations.}
\label{tab:request_coverage}
\end{table*}

Selection favors requests that are natural, situated in the \textsc{LifeMenu} setting, label-consistent, and dissimilar to existing candidates. It upweights undercovered categories and downweights repetitive openings, high-frequency phrases, and near-duplicates. The final set is reviewed for naturalness, scenario relevance, label consistency, coverage balance, and near-duplicate risk. Each request is paired with the full menu and sent to the selected teacher route to obtain parsed intent and a Top-$N$ recommendation list. The 1,000 examples are split into 800 training, 100 development, and 100 test examples after deduplication and coverage balancing, with disjoint sample IDs. We check near-duplicate phrasing and repeated high-level scenario--preference patterns to reduce template leakage between training and test. The test set is never used for student training or model selection. Appendix~\ref{app:data_synthesis} gives the full category inventory, synthesis rules, and split-quality checks.

Before training, we review the teacher-labeled data to verify menu-valid dishes, constraint preservation, and basic menu-grounded explanations. All 1,000 examples are within an acceptable quality range for supervision. We adapt Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with QLoRA on the 800 teacher-labeled training examples.

\subsection{Evaluated Systems}

The main evaluation includes six systems. Qwen Base is the unadapted Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model; Qwen QLoRA Student is our task-adapted local model. We compare it with four strong online LLM baselines: Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek-V3. All systems use the same 100 test requests, menu, prompt, inference script, output requirements, and post-processing format.

Each system is asked to output exactly $N$ menu-internal recommendations, with $N=5$. Each recommendation should contain a dish ID, dish name, and short explanation. All outputs entering human evaluation are normalized into the same display structure, so raters compare recommendation content rather than model-specific formatting. For human evaluation, non-schema outputs are normalized when recommended dishes and explanations can be identified; this normalization is used only for human scoring. Appendix~\ref{app:diagnostic} separately measures direct interface readiness without normalization.

For all systems, we use the same menu evidence, user request, task instruction, output requirement, and evaluation script. Decoding uses the same deterministic setting whenever supported by the model interface. We do not use model-specific prompt engineering, manual correction, or repeated sampling to improve outputs. When an API does not expose an identical decoding parameter, we use the closest deterministic or default setting and keep it fixed across test requests. Raw outputs are preserved before normalization for diagnostics.

\subsection{Blind Human Evaluation}

Human evaluation is the primary quality measure. Raters see a Chinese evaluation sheet containing the user request, difficulty label, and anonymized Top-$N$ outputs from multiple systems. System identities are replaced with labels such as System A, System B, and System C. Raters are instructed not to infer model identity and to score only visible recommendation content. Difficulty labels are used only for post-hoc analysis.

Six raters score each system output on a 1--5 scale. The score is holistic but task-specific, prioritizing recommendation quality over explanation fluency and following the order: hard constraints $>$ health goals $>$ taste preferences. A score of 5 means that the output satisfies the request well, obeys constraints, ranks dishes reasonably, and provides menu-grounded explanations. A score of 4 indicates only minor ranking or explanation issues. A score of 3 indicates partial satisfaction, missed preferences, or weak explanation. A score of 2 indicates multiple unsuitable recommendations or insufficient reasons. A score of 1 is assigned for clear hard-constraint violation, fabricated menu information, or largely irrelevant recommendations.

This protocol reflects the user-facing nature of the task: several ranked lists may be valid for the same request. Human evaluation is therefore the closest available proxy for practical recommendation quality. The holistic score is used because users see a complete ranked list, not isolated metrics. To reduce ambiguity, the rubric gives raters an explicit priority order: hard constraints, health goals, taste preferences, ranking quality, and menu-grounded explanation. We interpret the score as task-level utility rather than an isolated measure of explanation fluency.

\subsection{Blind Human Evaluation}

Human evaluation is the primary quality measure. Raters see a Chinese evaluation sheet containing the user request, difficulty label, and anonymized Top-$N$ outputs from multiple systems. System identities are replaced with labels such as System A, System B, and System C. Raters are instructed not to infer model identity and to score only visible recommendation content. Difficulty labels are used only for post-hoc analysis.

Six raters score each system output on a 1--5 scale. The score is holistic but task-specific, prioritizing recommendation quality over explanation fluency and following the order: hard constraints $>$ health goals $>$ taste preferences. A score of 5 means that the output satisfies the request well, obeys constraints, ranks dishes reasonably, and provides menu-grounded explanations. A score of 4 indicates only minor ranking or explanation issues. A score of 3 indicates partial satisfaction, missed preferences, or weak explanation. A score of 2 indicates multiple unsuitable recommendations or insufficient reasons. A score of 1 is assigned for clear hard-constraint violation, fabricated menu information, or largely irrelevant recommendations.

This protocol reflects the user-facing nature of the task: several ranked lists may be valid for the same request. Human evaluation is therefore the closest available proxy for practical recommendation quality. The holistic score is used because users see a complete ranked list, not isolated metrics. To reduce ambiguity, the rubric gives raters an explicit priority order: hard constraints, health goals, taste preferences, ranking quality, and menu-grounded explanation. We interpret the score as task-level utility rather than an isolated measure of explanation fluency.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4.2pt}
\begin{tabular}{lccccccc}
\toprule
Model & Overall & 95\% CI & Standard & Hard & Student $-$ Baseline & Paired 95\% CI & $p$ \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & \textbf{4.444 $\pm$ 0.064} & [4.318, 4.570] & 4.429 & 4.464 & -- & -- & -- \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 4.422 $\pm$ 0.061 & [4.302, 4.540] & 4.443 & 4.396 & +0.022 & [-0.134, 0.177] & 0.781 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 4.355 $\pm$ 0.069 & [4.219, 4.489] & 4.351 & 4.360 & +0.089 & [-0.090, 0.268] & 0.328 \\
GPT-4o & 4.278 $\pm$ 0.071 & [4.139, 4.415] & 4.319 & 4.227 & +0.166 & [-0.003, 0.335] & 0.054 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 4.200 $\pm$ 0.082 & [4.038, 4.359] & 4.214 & 4.182 & +0.244 & [0.046, 0.443] & 0.016 \\
Qwen Base & 3.108 $\pm$ 0.092 & [2.929, 3.288] & 3.086 & 3.136 & +1.336 & [1.132, 1.539] & $<0.00001$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Blind human evaluation on 100 test requests with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. Overall scores are averaged over six human raters. Student $-$ Baseline denotes the paired mean difference between Qwen QLoRA Student and the corresponding baseline. Positive values indicate a higher mean score for the student model.}
\label{tab:human_bootstrap}
\end{table*}

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{7pt}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Model & Valid req. & IntentHit@1 & IntentCoverage@5 & RankWeightedRel@5 \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & 94 & \textbf{0.6170} & \textbf{0.7730} & \textbf{0.5696} \\
GPT-4o & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7660 & 0.5173 \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7553 & 0.5096 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 94 & 0.5851 & 0.7553 & 0.5076 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 94 & 0.4787 & 0.7181 & 0.4842 \\
Qwen Base & 94 & 0.3936 & 0.6897 & 0.3554 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Automatic recommendation-ability diagnostics on the 94 test requests with detectable intent labels. IntentHit@1 measures whether the Top-1 dish matches the primary intent automatically extracted from the user request. IntentCoverage@5 measures the fraction of detected request intents covered by the union of the Top-5 dish labels. RankWeightedRel@5 is a rank-weighted request--dish relevance score, computed by weighting per-dish intent overlap with weights 1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, and 0.2 for ranks 1--5. These rule-based diagnostics do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth and are reported only as lightweight support for the human evaluation.}
\label{tab:auto_recommendation}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Main Results}

Table~\ref{tab:human_bootstrap} reports the main human evaluation results with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. The results show four main patterns.

First, the student model clearly improves over the unadapted local backbone. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the highest mean score, 4.444/5, while Qwen Base obtains 3.108/5. The paired difference is +1.336, with a 95\% paired bootstrap confidence interval of [1.132, 1.539] and $p<0.00001$. This large and stable gap shows that menu-task adaptation is essential for turning the base Qwen model into a usable \textsc{LifeMenu} recommendation engine.

Second, the student model is competitive with strong online LLM baselines. It has the highest observed mean score, but the margin over Claude Sonnet 4.5 is small: +0.022, with a paired 95\% confidence interval of [-0.134, 0.177] and $p=0.781$. The margins over Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-4o are also positive but not statistically stable at the 0.05 level. The student model shows a statistically supported advantage over DeepSeek-V3, with a paired difference of +0.244, a 95\% confidence interval of [0.046, 0.443], and $p=0.016$. We therefore interpret the result conservatively: the local student model is competitive with strong online LLMs under the same fixed-menu setting, with the highest observed mean score but only a small margin over the strongest online baseline.

Third, the student model remains stable across request difficulty. It scores 4.429 on 56 standard cases and 4.464 on 44 hard cases. Hard cases contain multiple preferences, negated constraints, health goals, missing menu targets, preference conflicts, or other trade-offs. The result suggests that the student model maintains strong recommendation quality when user intent becomes more complex.

Fourth, the automatic recommendation diagnostics in Table~\ref{tab:auto_recommendation} show a consistent supporting trend. These diagnostics are computed on the 94 test requests for which the keyword-based intent extractor detects at least one request intent. They do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth; instead, they compare the labels of recommended dishes with the intent labels extracted from the user request. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the best IntentHit@1, IntentCoverage@5, and RankWeightedRel@5 scores. This indicates that the adapted student is more likely to place a dish matching the primary request intent at the top of the list, to cover more of the detected request intents in its Top-5 recommendations, and to rank request-relevant dishes earlier. Since these metrics are rule-based proxies, we use them only as auxiliary evidence; the blind human evaluation remains the primary quality measure.

Qualitative inspection adds an important nuance. The student model is strong in dish selection and constraint matching, but its short explanations are often more concise and template-like than those of the online models. Claude, Gemini, and GPT-4o often provide more fluent and detailed explanations. The student result should therefore be read as strong holistic recommendation quality under the specified rubric, not as superiority in explanation expressiveness or general language ability.

We check near-duplicate phrasing and repeated high-level scenario--preference patterns to reduce template leakage between training and test. The test set is never used for student training or model selection. Appendix~\ref{app:data_synthesis} gives the full category inventory, synthesis rules, and split-quality checks.

Before training, we review the teacher-labeled data to verify menu-valid dishes, constraint preservation, and basic menu-grounded explanations. All 1,000 examples are within an acceptable quality range for supervision. We adapt Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with QLoRA on the 800 teacher-labeled training examples.

\subsection{Evaluated Systems}

The main evaluation includes six systems. Qwen Base is the unadapted Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model; Qwen QLoRA Student is our task-adapted local model. We compare it with four strong online LLM baselines: Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek-V3. All systems use the same 100 test requests, menu, prompt, inference script, output requirements, and post-processing format.

Each system is asked to output exactly $N$ menu-internal recommendations, with $N=5$. Each recommendation should contain a dish ID, dish name, and short explanation. All outputs entering human evaluation are normalized into the same display structure, so raters compare recommendation content rather than model-specific formatting. For human evaluation, non-schema outputs are normalized when recommended dishes and explanations can be identified; this normalization is used only for human scoring. Appendix~\ref{app:diagnostic} separately measures direct interface readiness without normalization.

For all systems, we use the same menu evidence, user request, task instruction, output requirement, and evaluation script. Decoding uses the same deterministic setting whenever supported by the model interface. We do not use model-specific prompt engineering, manual correction, or repeated sampling to improve outputs. When an API does not expose an identical decoding parameter, we use the closest deterministic or default setting and keep it fixed across test requests. Raw outputs are preserved before normalization for diagnostics.

\subsection{Blind Human Evaluation}

Human evaluation is the primary quality measure. Raters see a Chinese evaluation sheet containing the user request, difficulty label, and anonymized Top-$N$ outputs from multiple systems. System identities are replaced with labels such as System A, System B, and System C. Raters are instructed not to infer model identity and to score only visible recommendation content. Difficulty labels are used only for post-hoc analysis.

Six raters score each system output on a 1--5 scale. The score is holistic but task-specific, prioritizing recommendation quality over explanation fluency and following the order: hard constraints $>$ health goals $>$ taste preferences. A score of 5 means that the output satisfies the request well, obeys constraints, ranks dishes reasonably, and provides menu-grounded explanations. A score of 4 indicates only minor ranking or explanation issues. A score of 3 indicates partial satisfaction, missed preferences, or weak explanation. A score of 2 indicates multiple unsuitable recommendations or insufficient reasons. A score of 1 is assigned for clear hard-constraint violation, fabricated menu information, or largely irrelevant recommendations.

This protocol reflects the user-facing nature of the task: several ranked lists may be valid for the same request. Human evaluation is therefore the closest available proxy for practical recommendation quality. The holistic score is used because users see a complete ranked list, not isolated metrics. To reduce ambiguity, the rubric gives raters an explicit priority order: hard constraints, health goals, taste preferences, ranking quality, and menu-grounded explanation. We interpret the score as task-level utility rather than an isolated measure of explanation fluency.

\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4.2pt}
\begin{tabular}{lccccccc}
\toprule
Model & Overall & 95\% CI & Standard & Hard & Student $-$ Baseline & Paired 95\% CI & $p$ \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & \textbf{4.444 $\pm$ 0.064} & [4.318, 4.570] & 4.429 & 4.464 & -- & -- & -- \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 4.422 $\pm$ 0.061 & [4.302, 4.540] & 4.443 & 4.396 & +0.022 & [-0.134, 0.177] & 0.781 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 4.355 $\pm$ 0.069 & [4.219, 4.489] & 4.351 & 4.360 & +0.089 & [-0.090, 0.268] & 0.328 \\
GPT-4o & 4.278 $\pm$ 0.071 & [4.139, 4.415] & 4.319 & 4.227 & +0.166 & [-0.003, 0.335] & 0.054 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 4.200 $\pm$ 0.082 & [4.038, 4.359] & 4.214 & 4.182 & +0.244 & [0.046, 0.443] & 0.016 \\
Qwen Base & 3.108 $\pm$ 0.092 & [2.929, 3.288] & 3.086 & 3.136 & +1.336 & [1.132, 1.539] & $<0.00001$ \\

\caption{Blind human evaluation on 100 test requests with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. Overall scores are averaged over six human raters. \label{tab:human_bootstrap}

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{7pt}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Model & Valid req. & IntentHit@1 & IntentCoverage@5 & RankWeightedRel@5 \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & 94 & \textbf{0.6170} & \textbf{0.7730} & \textbf{0.5696} \\
GPT-4o & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7660 & 0.5173 \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7553 & 0.5096 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 94 & 0.5851 & 0.7553 & 0.5076 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 94 & 0.4787 & 0.7181 & 0.4842 \\
Qwen Base & 94 & 0.3936 & 0.6897 & 0.3554 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Automatic recommendation-ability diagnostics on the 94 test requests with detectable intent labels. IntentHit@1 measures whether the Top-1 dish matches the primary intent automatically extracted from the user request. IntentCoverage@5 measures the fraction of detected request intents covered by the union of the Top-5 dish labels. RankWeightedRel@5 is a rank-weighted request--dish relevance score, computed by weighting per-dish intent overlap with weights 1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, and 0.2 for ranks 1--5. These rule-based diagnostics do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth and are reported only as lightweight support for the human evaluation.}
\label{tab:auto_recommendation}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Main Results}

Table~\ref{tab:human_bootstrap} reports the main human evaluation results with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. The results show four main patterns.

First, the student model clearly improves over the unadapted local backbone. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the highest mean score, 4.444/5, while Qwen Base obtains 3.108/5. The paired difference is +1.336, with a 95\% paired bootstrap confidence interval of [1.132, 1.539] and $p<0.00001$. This large and stable gap shows that menu-task adaptation is essential for turning the base Qwen model into a usable \textsc{LifeMenu} recommendation engine.

Second, the student model is competitive with strong online LLM baselines. It has the highest observed mean score, but the margin over Claude Sonnet 4.5 is small: +0.022, with a paired 95\% confidence interval of [-0.134, 0.177] and $p=0.781$. The margins over Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-4o are also positive but not statistically stable at the 0.05 level. The student model shows a statistically supported advantage over DeepSeek-V3, with a paired difference of +0.244, a 95\% confidence interval of [0.046, 0.443], and $p=0.016$. We therefore interpret the result conservatively: the local student model is competitive with strong online LLMs under the same fixed-menu setting, with the highest observed mean score but only a small margin over the strongest online baseline.

Third, the student model remains stable across request difficulty. It scores 4.429 on 56 standard cases and 4.464 on 44 hard cases. Hard cases contain multiple preferences, negated constraints, health goals, missing menu targets, preference conflicts, or other trade-offs. The result suggests that the student model maintains strong recommendation quality when user intent becomes more complex.

Fourth, the automatic recommendation diagnostics in Table~\ref{tab:auto_recommendation} show a consistent supporting trend. These diagnostics are computed on the 94 test requests for which the keyword-based intent extractor detects at least one request intent. They do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth; instead, they compare the labels of recommended dishes with the intent labels extracted from the user request. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the best IntentHit@1, IntentCoverage@5, and RankWeightedRel@5 scores. This indicates that the adapted student is more likely to place a dish matching the primary request intent at the top of the list, to cover more of the detected request intents in its Top-5 recommendations, and to rank request-relevant dishes earlier. Since these metrics are rule-based proxies, we use them only as auxiliary evidence; the blind human evaluation remains the primary quality measure.

Qualitative inspection adds an important nuance. The student model is strong in dish selection and constraint matching, but its short explanations are often more concise and template-like than those of the online models. Claude, Gemini, and GPT-4o often provide more fluent and detailed explanations. The student result should therefore be read as strong holistic recommendation quality under the specified rubric, not as superiority in explanation expressiveness or general language ability.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4.2pt}
\begin{tabular}{lccccccc}
\toprule
Model & Overall & 95\% CI & Standard & Hard & Student $-$ Baseline & Paired 95\% CI & $p$ \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & \textbf{4.444 $\pm$ 0.064} & [4.318, 4.570] & 4.429 & 4.464 & -- & -- & -- \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 4.422 $\pm$ 0.061 & [4.302, 4.540] & 4.443 & 4.396 & +0.022 & [-0.134, 0.177] & 0.781 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 4.355 $\pm$ 0.069 & [4.219, 4.489] & 4.351 & 4.360 & +0.089 & [-0.090, 0.268] & 0.328 \\
GPT-4o & 4.278 $\pm$ 0.071 & [4.139, 4.415] & 4.319 & 4.227 & +0.166 & [-0.003, 0.335] & 0.054 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 4.200 $\pm$ 0.082 & [4.038, 4.359] & 4.214 & 4.182 & +0.244 & [0.046, 0.443] & 0.016 \\
Qwen Base & 3.108 $\pm$ 0.092 & [2.929, 3.288] & 3.086 & 3.136 & +1.336 & [1.132, 1.539] & $<0.00001$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Blind human evaluation on 100 test requests with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. Overall scores are averaged over six human raters. Student $-$ Baseline denotes the paired mean difference between Qwen QLoRA Student and the corresponding baseline. Positive values indicate a higher mean score for the student model.}
\label{tab:human_bootstrap}
\end{table*}

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{7pt}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Model & Valid req. & IntentHit@1 & IntentCoverage@5 & RankWeightedRel@5 \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & 94 & \textbf{0.6170} & \textbf{0.7730} & \textbf{0.5696} \\
GPT-4o & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7660 & 0.5173 \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7553 & 0.5096 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 94 & 0.5851 & 0.7553 & 0.5076 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 94 & 0.4787 & 0.7181 & 0.4842 \\
Qwen Base & 94 & 0.3936 & 0.6897 & 0.3554 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Automatic recommendation-ability diagnostics on the 94 test requests with detectable intent labels. IntentHit@1 measures whether the Top-1 dish matches the primary intent automatically extracted from the user request. IntentCoverage@5 measures the fraction of detected request intents covered by the union of the Top-5 dish labels. RankWeightedRel@5 is a rank-weighted request--dish relevance score, computed by weighting per-dish intent overlap with weights 1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, and 0.2 for ranks 1--5. These rule-based diagnostics do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth and are reported only as lightweight support for the human evaluation.}
\label{tab:auto_recommendation}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Main Results}

Table~\ref{tab:human_bootstrap} reports the main human evaluation results with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. The results show four main patterns.

First, the student model clearly improves over the unadapted local backbone. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the highest mean score, 4.444/5, while Qwen Base obtains 3.108/5. The paired difference is +1.336, with a 95\% paired bootstrap confidence interval of [1.132, 1.539] and $p<0.00001$. This large and stable gap shows that menu-task adaptation is essential for turning the base Qwen model into a usable \textsc{LifeMenu} recommendation engine.

Second, the student model is competitive with strong online LLM baselines. It has the highest observed mean score, but the margin over Claude Sonnet 4.5 is small: +0.022, with a paired 95\% confidence interval of [-0.134, 0.177] and $p=0.781$. The margins over Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-4o are also positive but not statistically stable at the 0.05 level. The student model shows a statistically supported advantage over DeepSeek-V3, with a paired difference of +0.244, a 95\% confidence interval of [0.046, 0.443], and $p=0.016$. We therefore interpret the result conservatively: the local student model is competitive with strong online LLMs under the same fixed-menu setting, with the highest observed mean score but only a small margin over the strongest online baseline.

Third, the student model remains stable across request difficulty. It scores 4.429 on 56 standard cases and 4.464 on 44 hard cases. Hard cases contain multiple preferences, negated constraints, health goals, missing menu targets, preference conflicts, or other trade-offs. The result suggests that the student model maintains strong recommendation quality when user intent becomes more complex.

Fourth, the automatic recommendation diagnostics in Table~\ref{tab:auto_recommendation} show a consistent supporting trend. These diagnostics are computed on the 94 test requests for which the keyword-based intent extractor detects at least one request intent. They do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth; instead, they compare the labels of recommended dishes with the intent labels extracted from the user request. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the best IntentHit@1, IntentCoverage@5, and RankWeightedRel@5 scores. This indicates that the adapted student is more likely to place a dish matching the primary request intent at the top of the list, to cover more of the detected request intents in its Top-5 recommendations, and to rank request-relevant dishes earlier. Since these metrics are rule-based proxies, we use them only as auxiliary evidence; the blind human evaluation remains the primary quality measure.

Qualitative inspection adds an important nuance. The student model is strong in dish selection and constraint matching, but its short explanations are often more concise and template-like than those of the online models. Claude, Gemini, and GPT-4o often provide more fluent and detailed explanations. The student result should therefore be read as strong holistic recommendation quality under the specified rubric, not as superiority in explanation expressiveness or general language ability.

\subsection{Evaluated Systems}

The main evaluation includes six systems. Qwen Base is the unadapted Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model; Qwen QLoRA Student is our task-adapted local model. We compare it with four strong online LLM baselines: Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek-V3. All systems use the same 100 test requests, menu, prompt, inference script, output requirements, and post-processing format.

Each system is asked to output exactly $N$ menu-internal recommendations, with $N=5$. Each recommendation should contain a dish ID, dish name, and short explanation. All outputs entering human evaluation are normalized into the same display structure, so raters compare recommendation content rather than model-specific formatting. For human evaluation, non-schema outputs are normalized when recommended dishes and explanations can be identified; this normalization is used only for human scoring. Appendix~\ref{app:diagnostic} separately measures direct interface readiness without normalization.

For all systems, we use the same menu evidence, user request, task instruction, output requirement, and evaluation script. Decoding uses the same deterministic setting whenever supported by the model interface. We do not use model-specific prompt engineering, manual correction, or repeated sampling to improve outputs. When an API does not expose an identical decoding parameter, we use the closest deterministic or default setting and keep it fixed across test requests. Raw outputs are preserved before normalization for diagnostics.

A score of 4 indicates only minor ranking or explanation issues. A score of 3 indicates partial satisfaction, missed preferences, or weak explanation. A score of 2 indicates multiple unsuitable recommendations or insufficient reasons. A score of 1 is assigned for clear hard-constraint violation, fabricated menu information, or largely irrelevant recommendations.

This protocol reflects the user-facing nature of the task: several ranked lists may be valid for the same request. Human evaluation is therefore the closest available proxy for practical recommendation quality. The holistic score is used because users see a complete ranked list, not isolated metrics. To reduce ambiguity, the rubric gives raters an explicit priority order: hard constraints, health goals, taste preferences, ranking quality, and menu-grounded explanation. We interpret the score as task-level utility rather than an isolated measure of explanation fluency.

Qwen QLoRA Student & \textbf{4.444 $\pm$ 0.064} & [4.318, 4.570] & 4.429 & 4.464 & -- & -- & -- \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 4.422 $\pm$ 0.061 & [4.302, 4.540] & 4.443 & 4.396 & +0.022 & [-0.134, 0.177] & 0.781 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 4.355 $\pm$ 0.069 & [4.219, 4.489] & 4.351 & 4.360 & +0.089 & [-0.090, 0.268] & 0.328 \\
GPT-4o & 4.278 $\pm$ 0.071 & [4.139, 4.415] & 4.319 & 4.227 & +0.166 & [-0.003, 0.335] & 0.054 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 4.200 $\pm$ 0.082 & [4.038, 4.359] & 4.214 & 4.182 & +0.244 & [0.046, 0.443] & 0.016 \\

\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{7pt}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Model & Valid req. & IntentHit@1 & IntentCoverage@5 & RankWeightedRel@5 \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & 94 & \textbf{0.6170} & \textbf{0.7730} & \textbf{0.5696} \\
GPT-4o & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7660 & 0.5173 \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7553 & 0.5096 \\

DeepSeek-V3 & 94 & 0.4787 & 0.7181 & 0.4842 \\
Qwen Base & 94 & 0.3936 & 0.6897 & 0.3554 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Automatic recommendation-ability diagnostics on the 94 test requests with detectable intent labels. IntentHit@1 measures whether the Top-1 dish matches the primary intent automatically extracted from the user request. IntentCoverage@5 measures the fraction of detected request intents covered by the union of the Top-5 dish labels. RankWeightedRel@5 is a rank-weighted request--dish relevance score, computed by weighting per-dish intent overlap with weights 1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, and 0.2 for ranks 1--5. These rule-based diagnostics do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth and are reported only as lightweight support for the human evaluation.}
\label{tab:auto_recommendation}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Main Results}

Table~\ref{tab:human_bootstrap} reports the main human evaluation results with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. The results show four main patterns.

First, the student model clearly improves over the unadapted local backbone. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the highest mean score, 4.444/5, while Qwen Base obtains 3.108/5. The paired difference is +1.336, with a 95\% paired bootstrap confidence interval of [1.132, 1.539] and $p<0.00001$. This large and stable gap shows that menu-task adaptation is essential for turning the base Qwen model into a usable \textsc{LifeMenu} recommendation engine.

Second, the student model is competitive with strong online LLM baselines. It has the highest observed mean score, but the margin over Claude Sonnet 4.5 is small: +0.022, with a paired 95\% confidence interval of [-0.134, 0.177] and $p=0.781$. The margins over Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-4o are also positive but not statistically stable at the 0.05 level. The student model shows a statistically supported advantage over DeepSeek-V3, with a paired difference of +0.244, a 95\% confidence interval of [0.046, 0.443], and $p=0.016$. We therefore interpret the result conservatively: the local student model is competitive with strong online LLMs under the same fixed-menu setting, with the highest observed mean score but only a small margin over the strongest online baseline.

Third, the student model remains stable across request difficulty. It scores 4.429 on 56 standard cases and 4.464 on 44 hard cases. Hard cases contain multiple preferences, negated constraints, health goals, missing menu targets, preference conflicts, or other trade-offs. The result suggests that the student model maintains strong recommendation quality when user intent becomes more complex.

Fourth, the automatic recommendation diagnostics in Table~\ref{tab:auto_recommendation} show a consistent supporting trend. These diagnostics are computed on the 94 test requests for which the keyword-based intent extractor detects at least one request intent. They do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth; instead, they compare the labels of recommended dishes with the intent labels extracted from the user request. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the best IntentHit@1, IntentCoverage@5, and RankWeightedRel@5 scores. This indicates that the adapted student is more likely to place a dish matching the primary request intent at the top of the list, to cover more of the detected request intents in its Top-5 recommendations, and to rank request-relevant dishes earlier. Since these metrics are rule-based proxies, we use them only as auxiliary evidence; the blind human evaluation remains the primary quality measure.

Qualitative inspection adds an important nuance. The student model is strong in dish selection and constraint matching, but its short explanations are often more concise and template-like than those of the online models. Claude, Gemini, and GPT-4o often provide more fluent and detailed explanations. The student result should therefore be read as strong holistic recommendation quality under the specified rubric, not as superiority in explanation expressiveness or general language ability.

Selection favors requests that are natural, situated in the \textsc{LifeMenu} setting, label-consistent, and dissimilar to existing candidates. It upweights undercovered categories and downweights repetitive openings, high-frequency phrases, and near-duplicates. The final set is reviewed for naturalness, scenario relevance, label consistency, coverage balance, and near-duplicate risk. Each request is paired with the full menu and sent to the selected teacher route to obtain parsed intent and a Top-$N$ recommendation list. The 1,000 examples are split into 800 training, 100 development, and 100 test examples after deduplication and coverage balancing, with disjoint sample IDs. We check near-duplicate phrasing and repeated high-level scenario--preference patterns to reduce template leakage between training and test. The test set is never used for student training or model selection. Appendix~\ref{app:data_synthesis} gives the full category inventory, synthesis rules, and split-quality checks.

Before training, we review the teacher-labeled data to verify menu-valid dishes, constraint preservation, and basic menu-grounded explanations. All 1,000 examples are within an acceptable quality range for supervision. We adapt Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with QLoRA on the 800 teacher-labeled training examples.

\subsection{Evaluated Systems}

The main evaluation includes six systems. Qwen Base is the unadapted Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model; Qwen QLoRA Student is our task-adapted local model. We compare it with four strong online LLM baselines: Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek-V3. All systems use the same 100 test requests, menu, prompt, inference script, output requirements, and post-processing format.

Each system is asked to output exactly $N$ menu-internal recommendations, with $N=5$. Each recommendation should contain a dish ID, dish name, and short explanation. All outputs entering human evaluation are normalized into the same display structure, so raters compare recommendation content rather than model-specific formatting. For human evaluation, non-schema outputs are normalized when recommended dishes and explanations can be identified; this normalization is used only for human scoring. Appendix~\ref{app:diagnostic} separately measures direct interface readiness without normalization.

For all systems, we use the same menu evidence, user request, task instruction, output requirement, and evaluation script. Decoding uses the same deterministic setting whenever supported by the model interface. We do not use model-specific prompt engineering, manual correction, or repeated sampling to improve outputs. When an API does not expose an identical decoding parameter, we use the closest deterministic or default setting and keep it fixed across test requests. Raw outputs are preserved before normalization for diagnostics.

\subsection{Blind Human Evaluation}

Human evaluation is the primary quality measure. Raters see a Chinese evaluation sheet containing the user request, difficulty label, and anonymized Top-$N$ outputs from multiple systems. System identities are replaced with labels such as System A, System B, and System C. Raters are instructed not to infer model identity and to score only visible recommendation content. Difficulty labels are used only for post-hoc analysis.

Six raters score each system output on a 1--5 scale. The score is holistic but task-specific, prioritizing recommendation quality over explanation fluency and following the order: hard constraints $>$ health goals $>$ taste preferences. A score of 5 means that the output satisfies the request well, obeys constraints, ranks dishes reasonably, and provides menu-grounded explanations. A score of 4 indicates only minor ranking or explanation issues. A score of 3 indicates partial satisfaction, missed preferences, or weak explanation. A score of 2 indicates multiple unsuitable recommendations or insufficient reasons. A score of 1 is assigned for clear hard-constraint violation, fabricated menu information, or largely irrelevant recommendations.

This protocol reflects the user-facing nature of the task: several ranked lists may be valid for the same request. Human evaluation is therefore the closest available proxy for practical recommendation quality. The holistic score is used because users see a complete ranked list, not isolated metrics. To reduce ambiguity, the rubric gives raters an explicit priority order: hard constraints, health goals, taste preferences, ranking quality, and menu-grounded explanation. We interpret the score as task-level utility rather than an isolated measure of explanation fluency.

DeepSeek-V3 & 4.200 $\pm$ 0.082 & [4.038, 4.359] & 4.214 & 4.182 & +0.244 & [0.046, 0.443] & 0.016 \\

\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{7pt}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Model & Valid req. & IntentHit@1 & IntentCoverage@5 & RankWeightedRel@5 \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & 94 & \textbf{0.6170} & \textbf{0.7730} & \textbf{0.5696} \\
GPT-4o & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7660 & 0.5173 \\

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Automatic recommendation-ability diagnostics on the 94 test requests with detectable intent labels. IntentHit@1 measures whether the Top-1 dish matches the primary intent automatically extracted from the user request. IntentCoverage@5 measures the fraction of detected request intents covered by the union of the Top-5 dish labels. RankWeightedRel@5 is a rank-weighted request--dish relevance score, computed by weighting per-dish intent overlap with weights 1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, and 0.2 for ranks 1--5. These rule-based diagnostics do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth and are reported only as lightweight support for the human evaluation.}
\label{tab:auto_recommendation}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Main Results}

Table~\ref{tab:human_bootstrap} reports the main human evaluation results with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. The results show four main patterns.

First, the student model clearly improves over the unadapted local backbone. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the highest mean score, 4.444/5, while Qwen Base obtains 3.108/5. The paired difference is +1.336, with a 95\% paired bootstrap confidence interval of [1.132, 1.539] and $p<0.00001$. This large and stable gap shows that menu-task adaptation is essential for turning the base Qwen model into a usable \textsc{LifeMenu} recommendation engine.

Second, the student model is competitive with strong online LLM baselines. It has the highest observed mean score, but the margin over Claude Sonnet 4.5 is small: +0.022, with a paired 95\% confidence interval of [-0.134, 0.177] and $p=0.781$. The margins over Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-4o are also positive but not statistically stable at the 0.05 level. The student model shows a statistically supported advantage over DeepSeek-V3, with a paired difference of +0.244, a 95\% confidence interval of [0.046, 0.443], and $p=0.016$. We therefore interpret the result conservatively: the local student model is competitive with strong online LLMs under the same fixed-menu setting, with the highest observed mean score but only a small margin over the strongest online baseline.

Third, the student model remains stable across request difficulty. It scores 4.429 on 56 standard cases and 4.464 on 44 hard cases. Hard cases contain multiple preferences, negated constraints, health goals, missing menu targets, preference conflicts, or other trade-offs. The result suggests that the student model maintains strong recommendation quality when user intent becomes more complex.

Fourth, the automatic recommendation diagnostics in Table~\ref{tab:auto_recommendation} show a consistent supporting trend. These diagnostics are computed on the 94 test requests for which the keyword-based intent extractor detects at least one request intent. They do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth; instead, they compare the labels of recommended dishes with the intent labels extracted from the user request. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the best IntentHit@1, IntentCoverage@5, and RankWeightedRel@5 scores. This indicates that the adapted student is more likely to place a dish matching the primary request intent at the top of the list, to cover more of the detected request intents in its Top-5 recommendations, and to rank request-relevant dishes earlier. Since these metrics are rule-based proxies, we use them only as auxiliary evidence; the blind human evaluation remains the primary quality measure.

Qualitative inspection adds an important nuance. The student model is strong in dish selection and constraint matching, but its short explanations are often more concise and template-like than those of the online models. Claude, Gemini, and GPT-4o often provide more fluent and detailed explanations. The student result should therefore be read as strong holistic recommendation quality under the specified rubric, not as superiority in explanation expressiveness or general language ability.

Student $-$ Baseline denotes the paired mean difference between Qwen QLoRA Student and the corresponding baseline. Positive values indicate a higher mean score for the student model.}
\label{tab:human_bootstrap}
\end{table*}

GPT-4o & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7660 & 0.5173 \\

Meal context & 7 types & lunch, dinner, post-workout eating, work or study meals, late meals, dessert after a meal \\
Request length & multiple buckets & controls variation from short user inputs to longer context-rich requests \\

\caption{Coverage design for the 1,000 controlled natural-language ordering requests. The dimensions are used to reduce template bias and cover realistic menu-ordering situations.}
\label{tab:request_coverage}
\end{table*}

Selection favors requests that are natural, situated in the \textsc{LifeMenu} setting, label-consistent, and dissimilar to existing candidates. It upweights undercovered categories and downweights repetitive openings, high-frequency phrases, and near-duplicates. The final set is reviewed for naturalness, scenario relevance, label consistency, coverage balance, and near-duplicate risk. Each request is paired with the full menu and sent to the selected teacher route to obtain parsed intent and a Top-$N$ recommendation list. The 1,000 examples are split into 800 training, 100 development, and 100 test examples after deduplication and coverage balancing, with disjoint sample IDs. We check near-duplicate phrasing and repeated high-level scenario--preference patterns to reduce template leakage between training and test. The test set is never used for student training or model selection. Appendix~\ref{app:data_synthesis} gives the full category inventory, synthesis rules, and split-quality checks.

Before training, we review the teacher-labeled data to verify menu-valid dishes, constraint preservation, and basic menu-grounded explanations. All 1,000 examples are within an acceptable quality range for supervision. We adapt Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with QLoRA on the 800 teacher-labeled training examples.

\subsection{Evaluated Systems}

The main evaluation includes six systems. Qwen Base is the unadapted Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model; Qwen QLoRA Student is our task-adapted local model. We compare it with four strong online LLM baselines: Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek-V3. All systems use the same 100 test requests, menu, prompt, inference script, output requirements, and post-processing format.

Each system is asked to output exactly $N$ menu-internal recommendations, with $N=5$. Each recommendation should contain a dish ID, dish name, and short explanation. All outputs entering human evaluation are normalized into the same display structure, so raters compare recommendation content rather than model-specific formatting. For human evaluation, non-schema outputs are normalized when recommended dishes and explanations can be identified; this normalization is used only for human scoring. Appendix~\ref{app:diagnostic} separately measures direct interface readiness without normalization.

For all systems, we use the same menu evidence, user request, task instruction, output requirement, and evaluation script. Decoding uses the same deterministic setting whenever supported by the model interface. We do not use model-specific prompt engineering, manual correction, or repeated sampling to improve outputs. When an API does not expose an identical decoding parameter, we use the closest deterministic or default setting and keep it fixed across test requests. Raw outputs are preserved before normalization for diagnostics.

\subsection{Blind Human Evaluation}

Human evaluation is the primary quality measure. Raters see a Chinese evaluation sheet containing the user request, difficulty label, and anonymized Top-$N$ outputs from multiple systems. System identities are replaced with labels such as System A, System B, and System C. Raters are instructed not to infer model identity and to score only visible recommendation content. Difficulty labels are used only for post-hoc analysis.

Six raters score each system output on a 1--5 scale. The score is holistic but task-specific, prioritizing recommendation quality over explanation fluency and following the order: hard constraints $>$ health goals $>$ taste preferences. A score of 5 means that the output satisfies the request well, obeys constraints, ranks dishes reasonably, and provides menu-grounded explanations. A score of 4 indicates only minor ranking or explanation issues. A score of 3 indicates partial satisfaction, missed preferences, or weak explanation. A score of 2 indicates multiple unsuitable recommendations or insufficient reasons. A score of 1 is assigned for clear hard-constraint violation, fabricated menu information, or largely irrelevant recommendations.

This protocol reflects the user-facing nature of the task: several ranked lists may be valid for the same request. Human evaluation is therefore the closest available proxy for practical recommendation quality. The holistic score is used because users see a complete ranked list, not isolated metrics. To reduce ambiguity, the rubric gives raters an explicit priority order: hard constraints, health goals, taste preferences, ranking quality, and menu-grounded explanation. We interpret the score as task-level utility rather than an isolated measure of explanation fluency.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4.2pt}
\begin{tabular}{lccccccc}
\toprule
Model & Overall & 95\% CI & Standard & Hard & Student $-$ Baseline & Paired 95\% CI & $p$ \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & \textbf{4.444 $\pm$ 0.064} & [4.318, 4.570] & 4.429 & 4.464 & -- & -- & -- \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 4.422 $\pm$ 0.061 & [4.302, 4.540] & 4.443 & 4.396 & +0.022 & [-0.134, 0.177] & 0.781 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 4.355 $\pm$ 0.069 & [4.219, 4.489] & 4.351 & 4.360 & +0.089 & [-0.090, 0.268] & 0.328 \\
GPT-4o & 4.278 $\pm$ 0.071 & [4.139, 4.415] & 4.319 & 4.227 & +0.166 & [-0.003, 0.335] & 0.054 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 4.200 $\pm$ 0.082 & [4.038, 4.359] & 4.214 & 4.182 & +0.244 & [0.046, 0.443] & 0.016 \\
Qwen Base & 3.108 $\pm$ 0.092 & [2.929, 3.288] & 3.086 & 3.136 & +1.336 & [1.132, 1.539] & $<0.00001$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Blind human evaluation on 100 test requests with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. Overall scores are averaged over six human raters. Student $-$ Baseline denotes the paired mean difference between Qwen QLoRA Student and the corresponding baseline. Positive values indicate a higher mean score for the student model.}
\label{tab:human_bootstrap}
\end{table*}

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{7pt}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Model & Valid req. & IntentHit@1 & IntentCoverage@5 & RankWeightedRel@5 \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & 94 & \textbf{0.6170} & \textbf{0.7730} & \textbf{0.5696} \\
GPT-4o & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7660 & 0.5173 \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7553 & 0.5096 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 94 & 0.5851 & 0.7553 & 0.5076 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 94 & 0.4787 & 0.7181 & 0.4842 \\
Qwen Base & 94 & 0.3936 & 0.6897 & 0.3554 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Automatic recommendation-ability diagnostics on the 94 test requests with detectable intent labels. IntentHit@1 measures whether the Top-1 dish matches the primary intent automatically extracted from the user request. IntentCoverage@5 measures the fraction of detected request intents covered by the union of the Top-5 dish labels. RankWeightedRel@5 is a rank-weighted request--dish relevance score, computed by weighting per-dish intent overlap with weights 1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, and 0.2 for ranks 1--5. These rule-based diagnostics do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth and are reported only as lightweight support for the human evaluation.}
\label{tab:auto_recommendation}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Main Results}

Table~\ref{tab:human_bootstrap} reports the main human evaluation results with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. The results show four main patterns.

First, the student model clearly improves over the unadapted local backbone. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the highest mean score, 4.444/5, while Qwen Base obtains 3.108/5. The paired difference is +1.336, with a 95\% paired bootstrap confidence interval of [1.132, 1.539] and $p<0.00001$. This large and stable gap shows that menu-task adaptation is essential for turning the base Qwen model into a usable \textsc{LifeMenu} recommendation engine.

Second, the student model is competitive with strong online LLM baselines. It has the highest observed mean score, but the margin over Claude Sonnet 4.5 is small: +0.022, with a paired 95\% confidence interval of [-0.134, 0.177] and $p=0.781$. The margins over Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-4o are also positive but not statistically stable at the 0.05 level. The student model shows a statistically supported advantage over DeepSeek-V3, with a paired difference of +0.244, a 95\% confidence interval of [0.046, 0.443], and $p=0.016$. We therefore interpret the result conservatively: the local student model is competitive with strong online LLMs under the same fixed-menu setting, with the highest observed mean score but only a small margin over the strongest online baseline.

Third, the student model remains stable across request difficulty. It scores 4.429 on 56 standard cases and 4.464 on 44 hard cases. Hard cases contain multiple preferences, negated constraints, health goals, missing menu targets, preference conflicts, or other trade-offs. The result suggests that the student model maintains strong recommendation quality when user intent becomes more complex.

Fourth, the automatic recommendation diagnostics in Table~\ref{tab:auto_recommendation} show a consistent supporting trend. These diagnostics are computed on the 94 test requests for which the keyword-based intent extractor detects at least one request intent. They do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth; instead, they compare the labels of recommended dishes with the intent labels extracted from the user request. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the best IntentHit@1, IntentCoverage@5, and RankWeightedRel@5 scores. This indicates that the adapted student is more likely to place a dish matching the primary request intent at the top of the list, to cover more of the detected request intents in its Top-5 recommendations, and to rank request-relevant dishes earlier. Since these metrics are rule-based proxies, we use them only as auxiliary evidence; the blind human evaluation remains the primary quality measure.

Qualitative inspection adds an important nuance. The student model is strong in dish selection and constraint matching, but its short explanations are often more concise and template-like than those of the online models. Claude, Gemini, and GPT-4o often provide more fluent and detailed explanations. The student result should therefore be read as strong holistic recommendation quality under the specified rubric, not as superiority in explanation expressiveness or general language ability.

Request length & multiple buckets & controls variation from short user inputs to longer context-rich requests \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Coverage design for the 1,000 controlled natural-language ordering requests. The dimensions are used to reduce template bias and cover realistic menu-ordering situations.}
\label{tab:request_coverage}
\end{table*}

Selection favors requests that are natural, situated in the \textsc{LifeMenu} setting, label-consistent, and dissimilar to existing candidates. It upweights undercovered categories and downweights repetitive openings, high-frequency phrases, and near-duplicates. The final set is reviewed for naturalness, scenario relevance, label consistency, coverage balance, and near-duplicate risk. Each request is paired with the full menu and sent to the selected teacher route to obtain parsed intent and a Top-$N$ recommendation list. The 1,000 examples are split into 800 training, 100 development, and 100 test examples after deduplication and coverage balancing, with disjoint sample IDs. We check near-duplicate phrasing and repeated high-level scenario--preference patterns to reduce template leakage between training and test. The test set is never used for student training or model selection. Appendix~\ref{app:data_synthesis} gives the full category inventory, synthesis rules, and split-quality checks.

Before training, we review the teacher-labeled data to verify menu-valid dishes, constraint preservation, and basic menu-grounded explanations. All 1,000 examples are within an acceptable quality range for supervision. We adapt Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with QLoRA on the 800 teacher-labeled training examples.

\subsection{Evaluated Systems}

The main evaluation includes six systems. Qwen Base is the unadapted Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model; Qwen QLoRA Student is our task-adapted local model. We compare it with four strong online LLM baselines: Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek-V3. All systems use the same 100 test requests, menu, prompt, inference script, output requirements, and post-processing format.

Each system is asked to output exactly $N$ menu-internal recommendations, with $N=5$. Each recommendation should contain a dish ID, dish name, and short explanation. All outputs entering human evaluation are normalized into the same display structure, so raters compare recommendation content rather than model-specific formatting. For human evaluation, non-schema outputs are normalized when recommended dishes and explanations can be identified; this normalization is used only for human scoring. Appendix~\ref{app:diagnostic} separately measures direct interface readiness without normalization.

For all systems, we use the same menu evidence, user request, task instruction, output requirement, and evaluation script. Decoding uses the same deterministic setting whenever supported by the model interface. We do not use model-specific prompt engineering, manual correction, or repeated sampling to improve outputs. When an API does not expose an identical decoding parameter, we use the closest deterministic or default setting and keep it fixed across test requests. Raw outputs are preserved before normalization for diagnostics.

\subsection{Blind Human Evaluation}

Human evaluation is the primary quality measure. Raters see a Chinese evaluation sheet containing the user request, difficulty label, and anonymized Top-$N$ outputs from multiple systems. System identities are replaced with labels such as System A, System B, and System C. Raters are instructed not to infer model identity and to score only visible recommendation content. Difficulty labels are used only for post-hoc analysis.

Six raters score each system output on a 1--5 scale. The score is holistic but task-specific, prioritizing recommendation quality over explanation fluency and following the order: hard constraints $>$ health goals $>$ taste preferences. A score of 5 means that the output satisfies the request well, obeys constraints, ranks dishes reasonably, and provides menu-grounded explanations. A score of 4 indicates only minor ranking or explanation issues. A score of 3 indicates partial satisfaction, missed preferences, or weak explanation. A score of 2 indicates multiple unsuitable recommendations or insufficient reasons. A score of 1 is assigned for clear hard-constraint violation, fabricated menu information, or largely irrelevant recommendations.

This protocol reflects the user-facing nature of the task: several ranked lists may be valid for the same request. Human evaluation is therefore the closest available proxy for practical recommendation quality. The holistic score is used because users see a complete ranked list, not isolated metrics. To reduce ambiguity, the rubric gives raters an explicit priority order: hard constraints, health goals, taste preferences, ranking quality, and menu-grounded explanation. We interpret the score as task-level utility rather than an isolated measure of explanation fluency.

GPT-4o & 4.278 $\pm$ 0.071 & [4.139, 4.415] & 4.319 & 4.227 & +0.166 & [-0.003, 0.335] & 0.054 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 4.200 $\pm$ 0.082 & [4.038, 4.359] & 4.214 & 4.182 & +0.244 & [0.046, 0.443] & 0.016 \\
Qwen Base & 3.108 $\pm$ 0.092 & [2.929, 3.288] & 3.086 & 3.136 & +1.336 & [1.132, 1.539] & $<0.00001$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Blind human evaluation on 100 test requests with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. Overall scores are averaged over six human raters. Student $-$ Baseline denotes the paired mean difference between Qwen QLoRA Student and the corresponding baseline. Positive values indicate a higher mean score for the student model.}
\label{tab:human_bootstrap}
\end{table*}

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4.2pt}
\begin{tabular}{lccccccc}
\toprule
Model & Overall & 95\% CI & Standard & Hard & Student $-$ Baseline & Paired 95\% CI & $p$ \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & \textbf{4.444 $\pm$ 0.064} & [4.318, 4.570] & 4.429 & 4.464 & -- & -- & -- \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 4.422 $\pm$ 0.061 & [4.302, 4.540] & 4.443 & 4.396 & +0.022 & [-0.134, 0.177] & 0.781 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 4.355 $\pm$ 0.069 & [4.219, 4.489] & 4.351 & 4.360 & +0.089 & [-0.090, 0.268] & 0.328 \\
GPT-4o & 4.278 $\pm$ 0.071 & [4.139, 4.415] & 4.319 & 4.227 & +0.166 & [-0.003, 0.335] & 0.054 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 4.200 $\pm$ 0.082 & [4.038, 4.359] & 4.214 & 4.182 & +0.244 & [0.046, 0.443] & 0.016 \\
Qwen Base & 3.108 $\pm$ 0.092 & [2.929, 3.288] & 3.086 & 3.136 & +1.336 & [1.132, 1.539] & $<0.00001$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Blind human evaluation on 100 test requests with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. Overall scores are averaged over six human raters. Student $-$ Baseline denotes the paired mean difference between Qwen QLoRA Student and the corresponding baseline. Positive values indicate a higher mean score for the student model.}
\label{tab:human_bootstrap}
\end{table*}

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{7pt}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Model & Valid req. & IntentHit@1 & IntentCoverage@5 & RankWeightedRel@5 \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & 94 & \textbf{0.6170} & \textbf{0.7730} & \textbf{0.5696} \\
GPT-4o & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7660 & 0.5173 \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7553 & 0.5096 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 94 & 0.5851 & 0.7553 & 0.5076 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 94 & 0.4787 & 0.7181 & 0.4842 \\
Qwen Base & 94 & 0.3936 & 0.6897 & 0.3554 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Automatic recommendation-ability diagnostics on the 94 test requests with detectable intent labels. IntentHit@1 measures whether the Top-1 dish matches the primary intent automatically extracted from the user request. IntentCoverage@5 measures the fraction of detected request intents covered by the union of the Top-5 dish labels. RankWeightedRel@5 is a rank-weighted request--dish relevance score, computed by weighting per-dish intent overlap with weights 1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, and 0.2 for ranks 1--5. These rule-based diagnostics do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth and are reported only as lightweight support for the human evaluation.}
\label{tab:auto_recommendation}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Main Results}

Table~\ref{tab:human_bootstrap} reports the main human evaluation results with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. The results show four main patterns.

First, the student model clearly improves over the unadapted local backbone. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the highest mean score, 4.444/5, while Qwen Base obtains 3.108/5. The paired difference is +1.336, with a 95\% paired bootstrap confidence interval of [1.132, 1.539] and $p<0.00001$. This large and stable gap shows that menu-task adaptation is essential for turning the base Qwen model into a usable \textsc{LifeMenu} recommendation engine.

Second, the student model is competitive with strong online LLM baselines. It has the highest observed mean score, but the margin over Claude Sonnet 4.5 is small: +0.022, with a paired 95\% confidence interval of [-0.134, 0.177] and $p=0.781$. The margins over Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-4o are also positive but not statistically stable at the 0.05 level. The student model shows a statistically supported advantage over DeepSeek-V3, with a paired difference of +0.244, a 95\% confidence interval of [0.046, 0.443], and $p=0.016$. We therefore interpret the result conservatively: the local student model is competitive with strong online LLMs under the same fixed-menu setting, with the highest observed mean score but only a small margin over the strongest online baseline.

Third, the student model remains stable across request difficulty. It scores 4.429 on 56 standard cases and 4.464 on 44 hard cases. Hard cases contain multiple preferences, negated constraints, health goals, missing menu targets, preference conflicts, or other trade-offs. The result suggests that the student model maintains strong recommendation quality when user intent becomes more complex.

Fourth, the automatic recommendation diagnostics in Table~\ref{tab:auto_recommendation} show a consistent supporting trend. These diagnostics are computed on the 94 test requests for which the keyword-based intent extractor detects at least one request intent. They do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth; instead, they compare the labels of recommended dishes with the intent labels extracted from the user request. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the best IntentHit@1, IntentCoverage@5, and RankWeightedRel@5 scores. This indicates that the adapted student is more likely to place a dish matching the primary request intent at the top of the list, to cover more of the detected request intents in its Top-5 recommendations, and to rank request-relevant dishes earlier. Since these metrics are rule-based proxies, we use them only as auxiliary evidence; the blind human evaluation remains the primary quality measure.

Qualitative inspection adds an important nuance. The student model is strong in dish selection and constraint matching, but its short explanations are often more concise and template-like than those of the online models. Claude, Gemini, and GPT-4o often provide more fluent and detailed explanations. The student result should therefore be read as strong holistic recommendation quality under the specified rubric, not as superiority in explanation expressiveness or general language ability.

All 1,000 examples are within an acceptable quality range for supervision. We adapt Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with QLoRA on the 800 teacher-labeled training examples.

\subsection{Evaluated Systems}

The main evaluation includes six systems. Qwen Base is the unadapted Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model; Qwen QLoRA Student is our task-adapted local model. We compare it with four strong online LLM baselines: Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek-V3. All systems use the same 100 test requests, menu, prompt, inference script, output requirements, and post-processing format.

Each system is asked to output exactly $N$ menu-internal recommendations, with $N=5$. Each recommendation should contain a dish ID, dish name, and short explanation. All outputs entering human evaluation are normalized into the same display structure, so raters compare recommendation content rather than model-specific formatting. For human evaluation, non-schema outputs are normalized when recommended dishes and explanations can be identified; this normalization is used only for human scoring. Appendix~\ref{app:diagnostic} separately measures direct interface readiness without normalization.

For all systems, we use the same menu evidence, user request, task instruction, output requirement, and evaluation script. Decoding uses the same deterministic setting whenever supported by the model interface. We do not use model-specific prompt engineering, manual correction, or repeated sampling to improve outputs. When an API does not expose an identical decoding parameter, we use the closest deterministic or default setting and keep it fixed across test requests. Raw outputs are preserved before normalization for diagnostics.

\subsection{Blind Human Evaluation}

Human evaluation is the primary quality measure. Raters see a Chinese evaluation sheet containing the user request, difficulty label, and anonymized Top-$N$ outputs from multiple systems. System identities are replaced with labels such as System A, System B, and System C. Raters are instructed not to infer model identity and to score only visible recommendation content. Difficulty labels are used only for post-hoc analysis.

Six raters score each system output on a 1--5 scale. The score is holistic but task-specific, prioritizing recommendation quality over explanation fluency and following the order: hard constraints $>$ health goals $>$ taste preferences. A score of 5 means that the output satisfies the request well, obeys constraints, ranks dishes reasonably, and provides menu-grounded explanations. A score of 4 indicates only minor ranking or explanation issues. A score of 3 indicates partial satisfaction, missed preferences, or weak explanation. A score of 2 indicates multiple unsuitable recommendations or insufficient reasons. A score of 1 is assigned for clear hard-constraint violation, fabricated menu information, or largely irrelevant recommendations.

This protocol reflects the user-facing nature of the task: several ranked lists may be valid for the same request. Human evaluation is therefore the closest available proxy for practical recommendation quality. The holistic score is used because users see a complete ranked list, not isolated metrics. To reduce ambiguity, the rubric gives raters an explicit priority order: hard constraints, health goals, taste preferences, ranking quality, and menu-grounded explanation. We interpret the score as task-level utility rather than an isolated measure of explanation fluency.

\midrule

GPT-4o & 4.278 $\pm$ 0.071 & [4.139, 4.415] & 4.319 & 4.227 & +0.166 & [-0.003, 0.335] & 0.054 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 4.200 $\pm$ 0.082 & [4.038, 4.359] & 4.214 & 4.182 & +0.244 & [0.046, 0.443] & 0.016 \\
Qwen Base & 3.108 $\pm$ 0.092 & [2.929, 3.288] & 3.086 & 3.136 & +1.336 & [1.132, 1.539] & $<0.00001$ \\

\caption{Blind human evaluation on 100 test requests with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. Overall scores are averaged over six human raters. \begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{7pt}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Model & Valid req. & IntentHit@1 & IntentCoverage@5 & RankWeightedRel@5 \\
\midrule
Qwen QLoRA Student & 94 & \textbf{0.6170} & \textbf{0.7730} & \textbf{0.5696} \\
GPT-4o & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7660 & 0.5173 \\
Claude Sonnet 4.5 & 94 & 0.5319 & 0.7553 & 0.5096 \\
Gemini 2.5 Pro & 94 & 0.5851 & 0.7553 & 0.5076 \\
DeepSeek-V3 & 94 & 0.4787 & 0.7181 & 0.4842 \\
Qwen Base & 94 & 0.3936 & 0.6897 & 0.3554 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Automatic recommendation-ability diagnostics on the 94 test requests with detectable intent labels. IntentHit@1 measures whether the Top-1 dish matches the primary intent automatically extracted from the user request. IntentCoverage@5 measures the fraction of detected request intents covered by the union of the Top-5 dish labels. RankWeightedRel@5 is a rank-weighted request--dish relevance score, computed by weighting per-dish intent overlap with weights 1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, and 0.2 for ranks 1--5. These rule-based diagnostics do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth and are reported only as lightweight support for the human evaluation.}
\label{tab:auto_recommendation}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Main Results}

Table~\ref{tab:human_bootstrap} reports the main human evaluation results with request-level paired bootstrap uncertainty. The results show four main patterns.

First, the student model clearly improves over the unadapted local backbone. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the highest mean score, 4.444/5, while Qwen Base obtains 3.108/5. The paired difference is +1.336, with a 95\% paired bootstrap confidence interval of [1.132, 1.539] and $p<0.00001$. This large and stable gap shows that menu-task adaptation is essential for turning the base Qwen model into a usable \textsc{LifeMenu} recommendation engine.

Second, the student model is competitive with strong online LLM baselines. It has the highest observed mean score, but the margin over Claude Sonnet 4.5 is small: +0.022, with a paired 95\% confidence interval of [-0.134, 0.177] and $p=0.781$. The margins over Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-4o are also positive but not statistically stable at the 0.05 level. The student model shows a statistically supported advantage over DeepSeek-V3, with a paired difference of +0.244, a 95\% confidence interval of [0.046, 0.443], and $p=0.016$. We therefore interpret the result conservatively: the local student model is competitive with strong online LLMs under the same fixed-menu setting, with the highest observed mean score but only a small margin over the strongest online baseline.

Third, the student model remains stable across request difficulty. It scores 4.429 on 56 standard cases and 4.464 on 44 hard cases. Hard cases contain multiple preferences, negated constraints, health goals, missing menu targets, preference conflicts, or other trade-offs. The result suggests that the student model maintains strong recommendation quality when user intent becomes more complex.

Fourth, the automatic recommendation diagnostics in Table~\ref{tab:auto_recommendation} show a consistent supporting trend. These diagnostics are computed on the 94 test requests for which the keyword-based intent extractor detects at least one request intent. They do not use teacher recommendations as ground truth; instead, they compare the labels of recommended dishes with the intent labels extracted from the user request. Qwen QLoRA Student obtains the best IntentHit@1, IntentCoverage@5, and RankWeightedRel@5 scores. This indicates that the adapted student is more likely to place a dish matching the primary request intent at the top of the list, to cover more of the detected request intents in its Top-5 recommendations, and to rank request-relevant dishes earlier. Since these metrics are rule-based proxies, we use them only as auxiliary evidence; the blind human evaluation remains the primary quality measure.

Qualitative inspection adds an important nuance. The student model is strong in dish selection and constraint matching, but its short explanations are often more concise and template-like than those of the online models. Claude, Gemini, and GPT-4o often provide more fluent and detailed explanations. The student result should therefore be read as strong holistic recommendation quality under the specified rubric, not as superiority in explanation expressiveness or general language ability.

Decoding uses the same deterministic setting whenever supported by the model interface. We do not use model-specific prompt engineering, manual correction, or repeated sampling to improve outputs. When an API does not expose an identical decoding parameter, we use the closest deterministic or default setting and keep it fixed across test requests. Raw outputs are preserved before normalization for diagnostics.

\subsection{Blind Human Evaluation}

Human evaluation is the primary quality measure. Raters see a Chinese evaluation sheet containing the user request, difficulty label, and anonymized Top-$N$ outputs from multiple systems. System identities are replaced with labels such as System A, System B, and System C. Raters are instructed not to infer model identity and to score only visible recommendation content. Difficulty labels are used only for post-hoc analysis.

Six raters score each system output on a 1--5 scale. The score is holistic but task-specific, prioritizing recommendation quality over explanation fluency and following the order: hard constraints $>$ health goals $>$ taste preferences. A score of 5 means that the output satisfies the request well, obeys constraints, ranks dishes reasonably, and provides menu-grounded explanations. A score of 4 indicates only minor ranking or explanation issues. A score of 3 indicates partial satisfaction, missed preferences, or weak explanation. A score of 2 indicates multiple unsuitable recommendations or insufficient reasons. A score of 1 is assigned for clear hard-constraint violation, fabricated menu information, or largely irrelevant recommendations.

This protocol reflects the user-facing nature of the task: several ranked lists may be valid for the same request. Human evaluation is therefore the closest available proxy for practical recommendation quality. The holistic score is used because users see a complete ranked list, not isolated metrics. To reduce ambiguity, the rubric gives raters an explicit priority order: hard constraints, health goals, taste preferences, ranking quality, and menu-grounded explanation. We interpret the score as task-level utility rather than an isolated measure of explanation fluency.

\centering
